segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Joshua Weissman, a man with long curly hair, glasses, and a black apron, speaking directly to the camera in a modern kitchen. With an enthusiastic and humorous tone, he introduces his plan to "make, taste, and rank the most popular dish in every single state in America," as quick cuts display various regional dishes like pizza, gumbo, and roasted chicken, followed by a map of the United States. He continues, describing the USA as a "melting pot of food and culture," noting that "everyone seems to think that their state has the best food," but humorously adding that "some just straight up have no food culture," specifically calling out "Delaware." This is accompanied by a montage of social media comments suggesting iconic state foods like 'Skyline Chili' and 'Cali burrito'. Joshua then explains the show's premise, stating that the audience voted for dishes to represent their states, which will be ranked on "the most famous tier list on the internet," from A through F, with a special "S-tier" for "truly God tier" dishes that will compete in a "showdown." With a sudden shift in tone, he excitedly declares, "But first, I have the most important announcement of the year. My cookbook is officially out today!" while briefly seen on a couch reading a tablet before returning to the kitchen. A dramatic, upbeat electronic music track kicks in as a title sequence flashes, showing a pixelated dish with a question mark before revealing the bold yellow 'DISH SHOWDOWN' graphic against a fiery background. Joshua reappears in the kitchen, now surrounded by multiple copies of his blue-covered cookbook titled 'joshua weissman: texture over taste'. He places one on a wooden cutting board, then celebrates exuberantly as golden confetti rains down, tossing the book in the air and dancing joyfully to the music. The segment concludes with him in a gray t-shirt, sitting at a small table with the cookbook, smiling and gesturing as if presenting it.

The man reappears in the kitchen, now with multiple copies of a cookbook titled 'joshua weissman: texture over taste' arranged on the counter and shelves. He places one on a wooden cutting board, then celebrates exuberantly as golden confetti rains down, tossing the book in the air and dancing joyfully. The final shot shows him in a different outfit, a gray t-shirt, sitting at a small table with the same cookbook, smiling and gesturing as if presenting it to the camera.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, celebratory track as Joshua Weissman, the Man with long curly hair and glasses, joyfully dances amidst golden confetti in his modern kitchen, proudly displaying his cookbook, 'joshua weissman: texture over taste'. He explains in his enthusiastic voice that he 'didn't want to make just another cookbook,' but rather 'the first cookbook of its time, focused on the one thing that no one ever talks about, which is texture,' featuring 'dozens of different recipes, techniques' like 'Nashville Hot Honey Karaage, brownies, greatest mac and cheese of your life.' After a close-up of the cookbook and a '75+ Recipes' graphic, he transitions to the video's content, introducing 'Utah Funeral Potatoes' as 'one of the strangest named dishes.' A graphic of Utah appears as he describes the dish as 'basically a cheesy potato casserole' with 'cornflakes, tossed in butter, lightly seasoned, put on top of a creamy, cheesy, shredded potato mixture,' baked for '1 hour.' He then sits with the Man in gray t-shirt, who rates the Funeral Potatoes a 'C,' stating 'it's not as bad as it looks,' while Joshua Weissman gives it a 'D,' adding it would be an 'F if it wasn't for the crunch on top,' concluding it's a 'D tier.' The scene shifts to a New Jersey graphic, where Joshua Weissman introduces the 'New Jersey Pork Roll Breakfast Sandwich,' mentioning the 'big argument about whether or not this is Taylor Ham' before describing the cooking of 'sliced pork product' and the assembly of the sandwich, highlighting a runny egg yolk.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of hands expertly assembling a Pork Roll Breakfast Sandwich, with a voiceover guiding the process: adding American cheese, cooking an egg in pork fat, seasoning it, and placing a fried egg on top before closing it up. The voiceover notes the cross-section "doesn't look too bad" as the sandwich is sliced, revealing a gooey interior. The scene transitions to a bright, modern living room where three men sit at a wooden table, tasting the sandwich. The man with long curly hair and glasses, wearing a black t-shirt with brown leather suspenders, initially declares, "That's an F," while chewing. The man in a gray striped hoodie reacts animatedly, exclaiming, "Oh my god, it really is that salty. Holy sh-" (bleeped). The man in a dark gray t-shirt adds, "It doesn't taste like ham. It tastes like preservative." The man in the gray striped hoodie then states, "I hate the Taylor Ham, but I love eggs, cheese and bread, so it's getting a C." The man with long curly hair and glasses reconsiders, saying, "The Taylor Ham's starting to grow on me. I'm still going to give it a C though." A graphic interface then highlights the New Jersey state graphic in yellow, as an energetic voiceover announces, "New Jersey lands at a C tier." The focus shifts to a bowl of Cincinnati-Style Chili over spaghetti, topped with shredded cheese, being served with a ladle. The voiceover introduces it as "Ohio Cincinnati-Style Chili," describing it as "Skyline can of chili over spaghetti with raw onion and shredded yellow cheddar." The men are shown tasting the chili with forks, displaying varied reactions. The man in the dark gray t-shirt, after a chew, apologetically says, "I uh, I'm sorry." The man in the gray striped hoodie gestures emphatically, stating, "To me, it smells like a white suburban mother's idea of charcuterie pork," and dramatically adds, "If an Italian saw this, I genuinely think that they would call the police immediately." The man in the dark gray t-shirt, despite being from Ohio, declares, "I was born in Ohio and I fully disagree with this." The man with long curly hair and glasses simply says, "Nah," while the man in the gray striped hoodie emphatically gives it "F, F." The man with long curly hair and glasses assigns it a "D." The man in the gray striped hoodie continues, "I think Ohio is like a beautiful state. If this ceased to exist, I would not care," as the group continues their discussion, with the chili bowl still on the table.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man with long curly hair and glasses, seated at the Dining table setting, declaring an 'F' rating for pasta. The Man in gray t-shirt then confirms an 'F tier' for Ohio, expressing sadness, before introducing the 'Minnesota Tater Tot Hotdish.' While he describes the dish as 'Great Depression era' with beef, onions, vegetables, cheese, and tater tots, the visual shows a creamy Funeral Potatoes casserole topped with crispy cornflakes being prepared, baked at 350° F, and presented. The Man in gray t-shirt notes it 'does look kind of nice,' and the Man in dark gray t-shirt adds, 'I'm here for the tots.' The group then rates the dish, with the Man with long curly hair giving a 'C' for being 'very middle of the line,' the Man in dark gray t-shirt suggesting a 'solid B,' and the Man in gray t-shirt concluding with an 'average C placement.' A graphic interface appears, highlighting New Jersey and Minnesota, as the Man in gray t-shirt introduces the 'Oklahoma Fried Onion Burger.' The visual shows the burger being assembled on a griddle with a beef patty, melted cheese, and crispy fried onions. After being sliced and tasted by the group, including the Man in gray t-shirt and the Man in dark gray t-shirt, the Man with long curly hair remarks that smashing the onions into the patty 'takes it up to here,' while the Man in dark gray t-shirt calls it 'something special.' All three men agree it's an 'S' tier, with the Man in gray t-shirt exclaiming, 'It's an S for me too. Too classy.' The graphic interface reappears, highlighting New Mexico, as the Man in gray t-shirt introduces the 'New Mexico Green Chili Cheese Burger,' which he notes the group 'voted for,' concluding with a close-up of the sizzling patty being pressed on a griddle.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a hand placing a slice of bread topped with melted cheese and green chilies onto a hot griddle, followed by a quick shot of a burger being assembled with a creamy sauce, as Cam, a man in a gray striped hoodie, provides a voiceover describing the process: "Smash patty, top with green chilies, top with cheese, melt the cheese... mayo up your bun, layer it up and we eat." The scene transitions to a bright, modern living room where three men are seated at a wooden dining table, actively engaged in conversation and tasting the food. Cam exclaims, "Man, it's good. It's good," and later rates the burger an 'A', acknowledging it "could be an F tier" but ultimately settling on an 'A' as the "average rating." The man in the dark gray t-shirt finds "a lot going on" and gives it an 'A' but feels it "could be improved upon." The man with long curly hair and glasses, however, rates it a 'B', stating it "tasted good" but was "not a fun experience." A graphic interface then appears, displaying a vertical rating scale with U.S. state outlines. Cam's voiceover introduces "number seven, Wisconsin fried cheese curds," as Wisconsin is highlighted and a bowl of golden-brown fried cheese curds is presented. The men react to the dish, with Cam covering his face in mock despair. The man in the dark gray t-shirt comments, "That's nice," but later rates them a 'B', comparing them to a "small mozzarella stick." The man with long curly hair and glasses expresses his strong affection, recalling how he was "scared to leave behind" cheese curds when he left the Midwest, declaring them "always an S in my book," and jokingly calling them a "chode mozzarella stick." Cam agrees, rating them an 'S'. The video then transitions to West Virginia, highlighted in white, followed by a close-up of a plate with a split bread roll and a small bowl of red sauce, as Cam introduces "West Virginia's official food, the pepperoni roll." The segment concludes with a hand kneading dough on a wooden board, preparing for the next dish.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of hands shaping dough, rolling it into a log, and placing it on a baking sheet before sliding it into a 400°F oven, as a male voice (Joshua Weissman) narrates the process of making pepperoni rolls, admitting he's 'guessing here' on the recipe. The scene transitions to a bright dining room where Joshua, wearing black-rimmed glasses and suspenders, holds up his cookbook, 'joshua weissman: texture over taste,' promoting it with 'LINK IN DESCRIPTION.' He then cuts the pepperoni roll, which he rates a 'C,' while a man in a gray striped hoodie gives it a 'B' and says he'd 'eat it again,' and a man in a dark gray t-shirt agrees but 'wouldn't be that excited.' Joshua concludes it's 'C tier.' The video then displays a graphic of North Carolina as Joshua introduces the next dish: a pulled pork sandwich with 'Lexington style barbecue sauce' containing vinegar, tomatoes, and spices, describing it as 'a wee bit spicy.' A close-up shows the sandwich being assembled with shredded meat, cheese, and sauce. segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a man with long curly brown hair, black-rimmed glasses, and a black t-shirt with brown leather suspenders, seated at a wooden dining table in a bright, modern living room, engaged in conversation with two other men. He declares North Carolina's dish a 'B tier' generally, which the man in the dark gray t-shirt agrees with, before the curly-haired man concludes, 'So that came in at a B tier.' The scene transitions to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states and ratings, as the curly-haired man announces, 'Number 10, Hawaiian Spam Musubi.' He explains his unique acceptance of Spam, stating, 'It is quite literally the only canned meat I accept because it's not canned meat. It's its own thing.' Visually, Spam slices are seared in a cast-iron skillet and then assembled into a musubi with rice and nori using a white plastic mold, as he narrates the process: 'You're gonna need a musubi mold... layer in some rice, some sweet soy glaze Spam... press it down, wrap that puppy up and split in half.' After cutting the musubi, he exclaims, 'I mean, it's beautiful, brother. I love...' and takes a bite with a satisfied expression, prompting an 'Oh!' from the man in the gray striped hoodie. He praises it as 'a perfect food,' while the man in the dark gray t-shirt notes its 'nice cross-section.' Despite finding it 'really hard,' he rates it 'a solid A tier,' with the man in the gray striped hoodie adding 'A plus plus' and 'So close to S.' He muses it would be an 'S if we were from Hawaii,' ultimately giving it 'an A,' concluding it's 'Coming in at a ripe A tier.' The scene then shifts to Wyoming on the graphic interface, and he introduces 'Elk,' acknowledging, 'If you're confused, so am I. This is what the people of Wyoming voted for.' A beautifully seared elk chop with a pat of butter is shown on a white plate as he describes it as 'a Wyoming elk steak, which is apparently pretty good for you. High in protein, zinc, B12, but contains a chef's worst nightmare: low fat.' He then lifts the chop to reveal its juicy interior before placing it back and using a torch to melt the butter, creating a golden glaze, while narrating, 'Seasoned it lightly, salt and pepper, seared it, and basted it with a nice herby garlic...' The video concludes with the glistening elk chop under the torch flame.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a lively scene at a wooden dining table in a bright, modern living room, where three men are engaged in animated conversation. The Man in dark gray t-shirt, gesturing expressively with his silver watch and ring visible, comments on the food being "pretty gamey" but that he likes it. The Man with long curly hair and glasses, listening intently, then interjects, stating he wants to give it a "D," elaborating that he doesn't "really eat game" and while he appreciates it, "for me it's enough." The Man in dark gray t-shirt adds his perspective, stating he likes "wild game" and it's "great," but to represent all the food they'll eat today, he rates it an "F." The Man in gray t-shirt, who had been listening thoughtfully, chimes in with "F for no fat." The Man in dark gray t-shirt clarifies that an "F" rating doesn't mean it's "bad," but rather a comparison to other foods, concluding it "just didn't taste that special." The scene transitions to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states with a vertical rating scale. A cursor selects 'Texas' in red, as the Man in dark gray t-shirt announces, "So let's F this up with number 12, Texas. This is where we live." He humorously warns against voting for "Waterburger" to represent Texas, threatening to "ship you out of here, brother," before revealing that the majority of votes for Texas went towards "brisket." A close-up of a perfectly seared elk chop on a white plate, topped with a pat of butter melting under a torch, is shown. The Man with long curly hair and glasses speaks about the "enjoyment of food," specifically "things melting in your mouth," and how "good brisket never fails to do that." The video cuts to a kitchen setting where a large cut of beef is seasoned, smoked, and then sliced to reveal a juicy, pink interior. The scene returns to the dining table, where the Man with long curly hair and glasses states he'd put it in the "A-tier," a sentiment echoed by the Man in gray t-shirt ("I'm going A as well"). The Man in dark gray t-shirt elaborates, suggesting it's "an A-tier" for Texans who eat smoked brisket regularly, but "an S-tier" for out-of-towners. The Man in dark gray t-shirt makes expressive hand gestures, and the Man with long curly hair and glasses nods. The graphic interface reappears, highlighting 'New Mexico' in yellow, as the Man in dark gray t-shirt concludes that "for us, we're giving brisket a solid, highly, highly rated..." The video ends with a close-up of a seared salmon fillet on a white plate, glistening with seasoning and a golden crust.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dark-themed graphic interface displaying U.S. states and a rating scale, as a male voice questions, "it out to what? A C?" A narrator then announces, "Number 17, Delaware," highlighting the state. The scene transitions to a package of 'Habersett Scrapple,' which the narrator explains is from Pennsylvania but loved by Delaware. The scrapple is sliced, pan-fried to a golden-brown crust, and served. Three men are seated at a wooden dining table in a bright living room. The man in the dark gray t-shirt exclaims, "Smelly Deli. Oh, God, I don't want to eat this. Gosh," and later vividly describes it as feeling like "throw up" before declaring it "F tier." The man with long curly hair and glasses takes a bite with a thoughtful expression. The narrator then introduces, "Number 18, Rhode Island, Calamari," despite a graphic of South Dakota appearing on screen. Fried calamari with peppers and onions is served, and the narrator describes it as "little fried calamari, tossed in a little garlic butter, some hot peppers." The man in the gray striped hoodie states, "I don't like calamari, but it kind of just tastes like anything fried." The man with long curly hair claps in approval after tasting it. The man in the dark gray t-shirt calls it "one of your best takes ever" but gives it a "strong powerful C." The man in the gray striped hoodie gives an "A" rating, mentioning he grew up in the Northeast, while the man with long curly hair rates it a "strong powerful B." The narrator then announces, "Number 19, South Dakota, Chislic," as a graphic of South Dakota reappears, followed by grilled meat skewers being prepared and served. The narrator questions saying the word 'Chislic' out loud and notes, "It's seasoned..." The men continue their discussion, with the man in the dark gray t-shirt gesturing expressively and the man in the gray striped hoodie listening thoughtfully.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of hands threading raw beef cubes onto a wooden skewer, which is then seared in a red Dutch oven, transforming into golden-brown Chislic. A male voice explains that this dish, often lamb, mutton, or venison, is seasoned and deep-fried. The scene transitions to a bright, modern dining table where three men are seated, engaging in animated discussion. The Man in dark gray t-shirt finds the Chislic "not bad," while the Man in gray t-shirt calls it "pretty good." The Man with long curly hair and glasses, gesturing expressively, rates it an "A," but the Man in dark gray t-shirt gives it a "solid B," noting there's "nothing wrong with it." The Man in gray t-shirt also gives a "B," suggesting a higher rating if a traditional accompaniment were present. The Man with long curly hair and glasses concludes their discussion, stating, "So we average out at a B." The scene transitions to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states with color-coded ratings, where a cursor selects Texas. A close-up of a creamy, yellow Knoephla Soup garnished with herbs and black pepper appears, with the text 'Knoephla Soup'. The Man with long curly hair and glasses introduces it as "Number 20, North Dakota," struggling to pronounce "Knoephla" ("Nofla, Knofla, Nofle"), admitting he's "not great with pronunciations." He describes it as a "creamy German chicken and dumpling soup," expressing a "good feeling about this one." The men return to the table, where the Man in dark gray t-shirt exclaims "God damn," and the Man in gray t-shirt says "Very good." The Man with long curly hair and glasses rates the Knoephla Soup an "A," elaborating that "it's creamy, it's salty, it's rich," and "surprisingly balanced," highlighting the "chewy dumplings" as its unique feature. He calls it "an A that aspires to be an S," to which the Man in dark gray t-shirt agrees to "keep it at an A tier." An 'Alabama' state graphic then confirms the rating. The segment shifts to a new dish: White BBQ Sauce, introduced by the Man with long curly hair and glasses as "Number 21, Alabama barbecue chicken with white barbecue sauce." He expresses surprise, having "always thought white barbecue sauce was illegal" due to his mom being from Texas, as the thick, creamy sauce is generously poured over grilled chicken pieces on a white plate, with the text 'White BBQ Sauce' displayed.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of golden-brown grilled chicken pieces being generously drizzled with a creamy white sauce, identified as 'White BBQ Sauce'. The Man in dark gray t-shirt describes it as a "nice smoked chicken" with a "mayo vinegar-based white barbecue sauce," speculating it keeps the chicken from drying out. The scene transitions to a bright, modern dining table where three men are seated. The Man in gray t-shirt, gesturing, declares the chicken "actually really good" and rates it an "A." The Man with long curly hair and glasses, with expressive hand movements, gives it a "B minus," noting it's "close to a C." The Man in dark gray t-shirt offers a lukewarm assessment, stating it's "good" but he "wouldn't care" if he never had it again, as an on-screen 'B' rating appears, confirming his "B tier" assessment. Next, a graphic of the state of Virginia appears as the Man in dark gray t-shirt introduces "Number 22, Virginia," explaining that "everybody voted for Country Ham." Hands are then shown assembling a biscuit sandwich, slicing a golden-brown biscuit and placing country ham inside, while the Man in dark gray t-shirt explains they decided to "give Virginia a helping hand" by putting the ham "on a sandwich," noting it's traditionally "served as a sandwich on a buttered biscuit." The Man with long curly hair and glasses, now with a green 'D' rating graphic overlaid on his chest, simply states "D." The Man in dark gray t-shirt responds, "I don't like that," elaborating that he would eat it "if I had to" but "only if I had to eat it." The Man with long curly hair and glasses exclaims, "Oh my god, I'm shocked, a D tier?", while the Man in gray t-shirt calmly replies, "I'm not shocked." The scene shifts to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states with color-coded ratings, where the cursor highlights Virginia, then transitions to Iowa as the Man in dark gray t-shirt introduces "Number 23, Iowa," identifying the dish as "the Iowa Chop." A plate of seared pork chops, labeled 'Iowa Chop', is shown as the Man in dark gray t-shirt describes it as "just a pork porterhouse," questioning why Iowa claims it. A cooking sequence follows, showing the chops seared in a cast-iron skillet with herbs and butter, as he details the process of searing, flipping, and basting with "butter, fresh thyme, rosemary, bay leaf." The chops are then finished with a rich brown sauce poured over them, and he concludes, "It's gonna be good, I mean, but it's just a pork chop." The final shot shows the plated Iowa Chop with on-screen text displaying various pronunciations ('Iowans', 'Iowahs', 'Iowoahs') as the Man in dark gray t-shirt warns about the pronunciation before tasting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a golden-brown, crispy pork tenderloin sandwich being assembled on a wooden cutting board, with the text 'lowahs' appearing on screen, followed by 'Pork Tenderloin Sandwich' as the top bun is placed, and a graphic of the state of Indiana emphasizing its regional origin. A male voice, later identified as the man with long curly hair, introduces the segment, stating, "Iowa, whatever they're called, I don't know, I'm sorry. Also voted for Pork Tenderloin Sandwich, unfortunately, that was way outvoted for Indiana, which we'll talk about next, but first, let's taste test Iowa." The scene transitions to a bright, modern living room where three men are seated at a wooden dining table. The man with long curly hair and glasses, wearing a black t-shirt with brown leather suspenders, speaks animatedly, giving the pork chop a "C," which the man in the dark gray t-shirt, wearing a silver watch and ring, echoes with "That's a C as well," and the man in the gray striped hoodie agrees, adding, "I agree with that," before the man with curly hair concludes, "A C-tier." The video then cuts to a close-up of a bowl of smoked salmon spread being prepared, with fresh herbs and a red sauce being added, followed by the spread being served on small toasted bread slices, identified by the text 'Smoked Salmon Spread' and an Alaska state graphic. During this, the man with long curly hair continues to narrate, describing the Indiana Pork Tenderloin Sandwich in detail, from its "bottom bun, yellow mustard, massive... comedically large piece of fried pork" to "raw onion, mustard on top bun, lid it and eat it," assuring viewers it's "a real thing." The men continue their conversation, with the man in the dark gray t-shirt enthusiastically describing the sandwich as "a fun eating experience" where "you could nibble on the outside and get just pork and then nibble on the inside get a sandwich," to which the man in the gray striped hoodie affirms, "It's a good sandwich," and the man in the dark gray t-shirt agrees, "It's a good sandwich." The man with long curly hair then rates the best version of the sandwich as a "B." As the Alaska Smoked Salmon Spread is shown, the man with long curly hair provides a detailed recipe, instructing to "mix cream cheese and salmon, add some sliced chives, brunoise shallots, season with Tabasco, Worcestershire, and lemon juice, a little salt, packed it into a ramekin beautifully." The setting remains consistent throughout, with the dining table and living room backdrop providing a warm, inviting atmosphere for the food discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a freshly baked, charred, and golden pizza on a wooden cutting board. A male voice, acting as a host, introduces the segment by stating, "We have an A tier," before specifying, "Number 29, Connecticut," as the cursor selects Connecticut on a graphic interface displaying U.S. states and ratings. He explains the pizza is "New Haven pizza," also pronounced "abits," and describes its preparation: a slightly more tart sauce, pecorino romano, and olive oil are added, noting it's "very, very, very light on the cheese." The pizza is then slid into a "super hot Gozney dome" to mimic Connecticut coal ovens, baking to a bubbly, blistered perfection. After removal, it's drizzled with olive oil and sliced, described as "charred, slightly crispy, yet foldable." The scene transitions to a dining table where three men taste the pizza. The Man in dark gray t-shirt, gesturing expressively, rates it a "D," explaining, "really what this is is a cheese pizza with less cheese than normal." The Man in gray t-shirt, resting his chin thoughtfully, also gives it a "D," humorously adding, "If you live in New Haven, just take the train to New York City. It's an hour away," followed by laughter. The Man with long curly hair and glasses, who initially introduced the segment, concurs, stating, "I think this is worth no more than a D," concluding with "A damning D tier." The host then transitions to "Number 30, Tennessee," introducing a new dish: "Nashville hot chicken." The crispy fried chicken leg, coated in a rich red sauce, is shown being sauced and assembled atop a slice of white bread with a pickle. The host describes it as "crackling fried chicken" tossed in "spicy hot seasoned oil," emphasizing, "You do not go light on the calories here," before it's plated. The final shot returns to the Man in gray t-shirt, now with a Connecticut state graphic superimposed over him, reinforcing the regional connection.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with three men seated at a wooden dining table in a bright, modern living room. The central figure, a man with long curly brown hair, black-rimmed glasses, and suspenders, speaks animatedly, audibly rating a previous dish with a 'C' tier, noting its 'comeback sauce' as an 'A' and 'fish' as a 'C'. To his left, a man in a dark gray t-shirt listens intently, while to his right, a man in a gray striped hoodie rests his chin thoughtfully. The scene transitions as the speaker introduces 'Number 33, Florida: The Cuban Sandwich,' describing it as one of the most famous and iconic sandwiches. The video then shows a close-up of the Cuban Sandwich being prepared: a long roll is sliced, mustard applied, and layers of shredded pork, ham, cheese, and pickles are assembled. The speaker details the ingredients, including 'Cuban bread, mustard, pickle, Swiss cheese, ham, slow cooked mojo pork shoulder,' before it's pressed until 'super flat, ultra crispy, melty delicious,' and sliced. The men return to the table, reacting with enthusiasm, the man in the dark gray t-shirt giving a thumbs-up. A graphic interface displays U.S. states and a rating scale, with Florida highlighted in red as the speaker rates the Cuban Sandwich an 'A' tier, calling it 'excellent' and 'better than most Cubanos I've had in Miami,' though he gives the overall dish a 'B' after '33 plates.' He then introduces 'Number 34, Massachusetts: Clam Chowder,' as a bowl of creamy New England Clam Chowder, garnished with bacon bits, fresh herbs, and butter, is shown and stirred, followed by a graphic of Massachusetts.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of hands garnishing a creamy New England Clam Chowder in a white speckled bowl with crispy bacon bits and fresh green herbs. A male voice, later identified as Vikram, describes it as an "OG" dish, "creamy," "sweet," and garnished with "crispy chopped bacon, parsley, cracked pepper," calling it one of his "favorite soups." The scene transitions to a bright, modern living room where Vikram, wearing a black t-shirt and brown leather suspenders, is seated at a wooden dining table with two other men. Vikram expresses his love for the soup, while the Man in dark gray t-shirt, wearing a silver watch and ring, reveals he's "never had clam chowder before" but is "excited." Vikram passionately rates the chowder as an "S," calling it "a classic" and "so good," with the Man in gray t-shirt, in a striped hoodie, agreeing with the "S" rating. The Man in dark gray t-shirt, however, gives it an "A." Vikram then debates the "S" rating in the context of other soups like chicken soup and gumbo, concluding, "the S's have it." The scene cuts to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states with color-coded ratings, where a cursor briefly highlights the Connecticut state graphic in blue, as Vikram introduces the next dish: "Number 35, Kentucky. Hot Brown." The video shifts to a kitchen setting, showing the preparation of the Hot Brown, which Vikram describes as looking like a "demonic creature" from a fantasy movie, originating from "The Brown Hotel." He details its components: slices of bread, raw tomato halves, a thick slice of turkey, covered with a "mornay sauce," or "cheese sauce," then sprinkled with pecorino cheese, broiled, and finished with bacon, paprika, and parsley. The scene returns to the dining table, where the Man in dark gray t-shirt questions, "How do you eat this?" Vikram explains how "the cheese melts really well with the turkey," as a large yellow 'A' rating graphic appears over Vikram, indicating his positive evaluation of the Hot Brown.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in dark gray t-shirt, animatedly speaking with his hands clasped, wearing a silver watch and a ring, in a bright, modern living room. He comments, "It'd be a really good sandwich," and questions, "Why can't they just make this a sandwich?" The scene transitions to a wider shot showing him seated at the wooden dining table with the Man with long curly hair and glasses and the Man in gray t-shirt, all engaged in conversation. The Man with long curly hair and glasses adds, "It tastes good," while the Man in gray t-shirt agrees, "It does taste good, but it's a mess." All three men then give a "C" rating to the previous dish, with the Man in dark gray t-shirt stating, "I'm gonna give that a C," the Man with long curly hair and glasses adding, "I'm gonna give it a C as well," and the Man in gray t-shirt concluding, "I'm gonna give it a C also," followed by an enthusiastic "That's an easy C." A narrator then introduces "Number 36, Idaho," as a graphic interface with U.S. states and ratings appears, highlighting New Jersey in yellow, while the narrator reveals the next dish: "Battered steak fingers." A close-up of creamy mashed potatoes being stirred with a fork is shown, followed by a black screen with the text 'guess what was' before revealing 'Steak Fingers' as the dish name. A person in black gloves removes golden-brown, crispy steak fingers from a deep fryer and places them on a plate. The Man with long curly hair and glasses expresses his initial dislike, "I don't like the way that these look," describing them as "essentially like a tempura battered piece of steak that gets deep fried." The Man in dark gray t-shirt humorously interjects, "I just farted. So if you smell that, I'm so sorry," prompting the Man with long curly hair and glasses to react with "Oh god, dude, that's bad," and the Man in dark gray t-shirt to quip, "We didn't even eat the steak fingers yet." The three men are shown again, with the Man with long curly hair and glasses holding a steak finger and speaking enthusiastically about the recipe's origin, "Supposedly this is the recipe that comes from the place that created the Idaho steak finger." He then critiques the dish, stating, "What I don't like about it is that it's a batter and I just don't think that that's gonna do well with uh, steak," and later adds, "Batter aside, this steak is so well done. I don't know how frying this was gonna make it any better." The Man in dark gray t-shirt reacts with a smile and a thumbs-up, but then gives his rating, "I'm gonna go with an F for this," which the Man with long curly hair and glasses echoes, "I'm gonna give this a solid F," before advising, "I'm really sorry Idaho. Maybe leave with a potato dish next time." The video then cuts to a black screen with 'Bierock' and 'Runza' text, as the narrator announces, "Now we're moving to the Midwest to try Bierock from Kansas for number 37 and Runza from Nebraska for number 38," adding, "That's two for one, they're both very similar. With beef..." A split-screen shows golden-brown buns on a plate, with the top half labeled 'Bierock' and the bottom half labeled 'Runza'. The final shot is a close-up of the buns, emphasizing their soft, flaky texture and golden crust.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of hands holding a split Bierock, revealing its savory meat and cabbage filling, as a male voice describes it as 'cabbage-filled pastry pockets' and explains the difference between Kansas Bierock, which has 'straight cooked cabbage,' and Nebraska Runza, which contains 'sauerkraut.' The text 'Bierock' is displayed. The scene transitions to a white plate with three golden-brown Runzas, labeled 'Runza', as the voice reiterates 'Kansas Bierock.' Three men are then shown seated at a wooden dining table in a bright, modern living room. The Man with long curly hair and glasses, wearing a black t-shirt and brown leather suspenders, takes a bite of a Runza, remarking, 'I've never heard of this, really reminds me of like a kolache or a klobasnek.' He continues, 'I mean it's good, not a lot going on. This feels very necessity food, like, this is all you got. I'm gonna give it a C.' The Man in dark gray t-shirt then takes a bite, agreeing, 'I'll go C as well,' followed by the Man in gray t-shirt, who chews thoughtfully and also says, 'C.' A graphic interface with U.S. states and ratings appears, with the cursor selecting 'Kansas' for the Runza, as the Man with long curly hair and glasses concludes, 'Could be better. Kansas C-tier.' A narrator then prompts, 'Now, let's see how the Nebraska Runza fares.' The Man in dark gray t-shirt expresses, 'I'm not about the sauerkraut. That's why I'd give it a C.' The Man with long curly hair and glasses then comments on 'The acidity... really like that. It's a slight step up from Kansas, but it's very similar, so I'll give it a B.' The Man in gray t-shirt agrees, 'I agree. I'll go with a B also,' and the Man in dark gray t-shirt confirms, 'We're going with B.' The scene shifts to a close-up of a plate of Lemon Pepper Wet Wings, with a spoon drizzling sauce over them, as a narrator introduces 'Number 39, Georgia. A place plentiful of incredible food. Georgia, with Lemon Pepper Wet Wings.' The narrator details the preparation, stating, 'This uses my lemon pepper wet recipe, so we deep-fried our wings, we tossed them beautifully in our sauce, garnished with a little bit of flaky salt. You know, it's nice.' The text 'Lemon Pepper Wet Wings' is displayed. The Man in dark gray t-shirt gestures enthusiastically, exclaiming, 'Easy S-tier!' The Man with long curly hair and glasses adjusts his glasses, adding, 'S. Tangy, crispy, flavorful.' The Man in gray t-shirt calls it 'an ultimate wing.' The Man with long curly hair and glasses further praises, 'After the second chew my brain immediately went S. S-tier.' The graphic interface reappears, with the cursor selecting 'Georgia' for the wings. The Man in gray t-shirt is shown with a 'B' rating graphic, and the Man with long curly hair and glasses receives an 'A' rating graphic. The video ends with a close-up of the Lemon Pepper Wet Wings being sauced, followed by the Man in dark gray t-shirt giving a thumbs-up and saying, 'Georgia, you're always on my mind.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a bright, modern living room where three men are seated at a wooden dining table. The central figure, a man with long curly brown hair, black-rimmed glasses, and a black t-shirt with brown leather suspenders, gestures animatedly while speaking, discussing how a dish is "always going to be capped at a good, nice strong B." A male voiceover then introduces "Number 47, Oregon, Marionberry Pie." The man with curly hair describes the pie as "the most Oregon pie" because it contains berries "literally invented... in Oregon," before stating, "I don't like the texture of this." A close-up shows the Marionberry Pie on a plate with 'Marionberry Pie' and 'OREGON' text. The man with curly hair raises a fork, takes a bite, and after a scraping sound, declares it "Good," but then rates it a "C" for a "very average pie experience." The man in the dark gray t-shirt agrees, stating, "I'm also gonna give it a C," while the man in the gray striped hoodie adds, "To me this pie was pretty one note," though he praises the "great" and "nicely seasoned" crust with good sugar levels. The man with curly hair then changes his rating to a "D" from a "flavor perspective," explaining that the pie relies solely on the Marionberry, concluding, "That's a D pie." The scene transitions to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states with color-coded ratings, where a cursor selects 'ILLINOIS' in red. The voiceover announces "Number 48, Illinois," explaining that "The Italian beef sandwich beat out Chicago hot dogs by two whole votes. And deep dish pizza by one vote," declaring, "it's Mr. Beef time, baby." A close-up shows a Chicago-style Italian Beef sandwich being assembled with roast beef, giardiniera, and peppers, with 'ILLINOIS' text. The voiceover describes the preparation: "thinly sliced roast beef heated in jus, placed in a roll, topped with a relish called giardiniera. The whole sandwich is heavily dipped in the jus." The video cuts to the exterior of 'Mr. BEEF Est. 1979', followed by a split-screen showing the sandwich preparation and the men reacting with interest. The final frames show the sandwich being assembled with a spoonful of giardiniera, displaying the text 'The Greatest Roast Beef Sandwich Ever (Italian Beef)', and concluding with a podium graphic ranking the sandwich as #1, above a burger and hot dog.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens in a bright, modern living room where three men, Josh, Cam, and Vicram, are seated at a wooden dining table for a food tasting competition. Josh, in a black t-shirt and suspenders, initially holds a '1.0' card, while Cam, in a gray striped hoodie, holds a '2.0' card, and Vicram, in a dark gray t-shirt, holds a '5.0' card. The group discusses the 'Arkansas' score, with Josh commenting on it being "the biggest accomplishment for Arkansas this year," followed by laughter. Cam expresses his positive surprise, saying he "liked it so much more than I thought I was going to be," while Josh humorously adds he's "still upset that it exists." Vicram offers a nuanced positive review, finding a dish "very inventive" and praising its "combination." On-screen graphics show Arkansas with a total score of 7 points, which Josh confirms as "strong 7 points." The focus then shifts to the 'Smoked Salmon Spread,' introduced with an Alaska state graphic and close-ups of the dish. Josh announces the scores for the salmon dip: Vicram's score is announced as 'one,' though he is later seen revealing a '7.0' card for a different dish. Josh's own score is 'five,' matching the '5.0' card he later reveals. Cam's score is 'two,' matching the '2.0' card he displays, prompting Cam to exclaim, "What the f***, guys." Josh then gives a sarcastic review, stating, "It tastes incredible. There's not one ounce of anything special about this." Vicram, however, praises the salmon dip for its "essence and enjoyment," while Cam expresses a preference for "other smoked fish dips." Josh summarizes Alaska's score as "eight points." The next dish, 'Pork Chile Verde Stew,' is introduced with a close-up, and Josh reacts to Vicram's '7.0' score with "Jesus, a seven?" He then announces Cam's score as 'three,' despite Cam being visually seen holding a '2.0' card, and sets the stakes, noting they "need a 9.5 or higher to take the lead." Vicram enthusiastically describes the chili verde as "such a good way for green chili to come through," adding he'd "actually go to the state and ask for that," a sentiment Josh immediately contradicts, stating, "I would not go to the state and ask for it." The segment concludes with a ranking graphic showing California in first place with 19.5, Alaska with 8, and Arkansas with 7, as the men continue their lively and competitive discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a bright, modern living room where three men are seated at a wooden dining table, holding score cards. The Man in dark gray t-shirt, identified as Cam, reveals a '7' for the hearty shredded meat sandwich, stating that it "does not need and should never have cheese" and is "good on its own." The Man with long curly hair and glasses, Vicram, then reveals a '9', praising the sandwich as inventive, tangy, sweet, and "the top sandwich for me." The Man in gray t-shirt shows a '6', commenting that "an Italian beef is one of the most well-balanced sandwiches you can eat." On-screen graphics confirm their scores: 'CAM 7.0' and 'Vicram 9.0', alongside a 'Vicram 10' graphic. The scene cuts to a close-up of the shredded meat sandwich being assembled, followed by a ranking graphic displaying U.S. states and their scores for the sandwiches, with a voice announcing "Illinois 24 points" as a "new leader." The focus then shifts to the "Oklahoma Fried Onion Burger," with a voice setting up the next round. A close-up shows the burger, featuring a beef patty, melted cheese, and creamy sauce, being assembled on a wooden cutting board. Vicram is shown again, now with a '9.0' score for the burger, and a voice declares, "Vicram, a nine. Mine, a nine." The voice adds that "we need a seven or higher to win this" and notes that "Cam has not been the nicest." Cam then scores a '7' for the burger, leading to the announcement, "We have a winner, the Oklahoma Fried Onion Burger with 25 points." The voice expresses, "I crave this item by itself." The Man in gray t-shirt offers his opinion on the burger, calling it "an amazing burger, but did it reinvent the wheel? No." Vicram enthusiastically praises the burger, stating, "Listen, it's America. There's nothing better than a burger. This is definitely one of the top ways to make a burger. You just can't beat melted cheese, grilled onions, and a smash patty." The final shot reveals the completed burger with a bold 'WINNER' graphic and the Oklahoma state flag, as a voice concludes, "The burger might not have been created in America, but the burger was owned by America."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man with long curly brown hair, black-rimmed glasses, and a black t-shirt with brown leather suspenders, seated at a wooden dining table in a bright, modern living room. He speaks directly to the camera with an enthusiastic and clear voice, gesturing expressively, stating, "it feels righteous that it won today." He continues, discussing the prevalence of "great food all over the United States of America," encouraging viewers to "venture out, try your local restaurants, try restaurants all over the US." He thanks "everyone who submitted your favorite food in your state," acknowledging their contribution to the video. He then holds up a blue-covered cookbook titled 'joshua weissman: texture over taste', promoting it by saying, "And don't forget my cookbook is officially out and there are recipes in this very video that we use. It's available now, go and get it, the link's in the description, click it, order it, it helps us a ton." He emphasizes the book's theme, declaring, "It's all about that texture, baby. Not just flavor." A close-up shot then shows hands slicing a juicy, pink-centered piece of smoked beef on a wooden cutting board, emphasizing its texture. Returning to the man, he continues speaking while holding the cookbook, and the text 'LINK IN DESCRIPTION' appears in bold yellow letters across the screen. He concludes his monologue, saying, "So with that being said, if you enjoyed this video or you learned something, leave a like, subscribe, and I will see you next time." The scene then shifts to include two other men seated at the table with him: one in a dark gray t-shirt and another in a gray striped hoodie. All three are engaged in a food tasting, with the man in the dark gray t-shirt eating, the man in the gray hoodie observing thoughtfully, and the man with long curly hair holding a small white plate with a half-eaten sandwich, possibly a pork roll breakfast sandwich, and a knife. Upbeat, modern, slightly funky instrumental background music plays throughout this segment, creating a casual and conversational atmosphere as the men react to and discuss the food.
